title: AI Agents: What Could Go Wrong

modules.title: What AI Agents Actually Are and Why Everyone's Building Them
modules.sub: Understanding what makes an agent different from a chatbot
modules.description: Learners build a clear mental model of what AI agents are, how they differ from standard AI tools, and why they are being adopted so rapidly. By the end, learners can confidently explain what an agent does in plain language and why that distinction matters for safety.

modules.title: Failure Modes: How Agents Go Off the Rails
modules.sub: Mapping the ways agents misinterpret, overstep, or spiral
modules.description: Learners explore the core categories of agent failure, from misunderstood instructions to runaway task loops. They practice recognizing each failure type through scenarios and begin connecting failure patterns to real-world consequences.

modules.title: Real Incidents: Cases Where AI Agents Caused Real Harm
modules.sub: Examining documented cases where things actually went wrong
modules.description: Learners analyze real and reported incidents where AI agents produced harmful, embarrassing, or costly outcomes. They identify what went wrong at each stage and extract lessons that apply to their own potential use of agents.

modules.title: The Control Problem: How Humans Stay in the Loop
modules.sub: Deciding when to hand off control and when to hold it
modules.description: Learners examine the tension between automation and human oversight, and explore what meaningful control actually looks like in practice. They evaluate different oversight designs and decide which levels of autonomy are appropriate for different types of tasks.

modules.title: Evaluating an Agent Before You Trust It With Anything
modules.sub: Running a structured check before giving an agent real access
modules.description: Learners apply a practical evaluation framework to assess an agent's permissions, transparency, and track record before deploying it. They work through a guided checklist and make a go or no-go decision on a sample agent setup.

modules.title: Safety Red Flags Every User Should Know
modules.sub: Spotting the warning signs that an agent setup is risky
modules.description: Learners build a personal radar for detecting unsafe agent configurations, including vague permission scopes, no audit trail, and lack of rollback options. They practice flagging red flags in realistic scenarios and decide what action to take in each case.

modules.title: Who's Responsible When an Agent Messes Up?
modules.sub: Tracing accountability across users, developers, and platforms
modules.description: Learners untangle the overlapping responsibilities of the people and organizations involved when an agent causes harm. They apply an accountability framework to case scenarios and articulate clearly who bears responsibility and why.

modules.title: Applying a Safety Lens to Your Own AI Use
modules.sub: Turning course instincts into a personal safety habit
modules.description: Learners audit their own current or planned use of AI agents using everything covered in the course. They produce a short personal safety plan that identifies their highest-risk touchpoints and the specific checks they will apply going forward.

synopsis: AI agents are being handed more and more real-world tasks â browsing the web, sending emails, managing files â and most people using them have no idea what happens when they go wrong. This course gives you the mental models and practical instincts to use agents confidently without getting burned.
rationale: As agentic AI tools become mainstream in workplaces and side projects, young adults need practical safety literacy â not just abstract ethics â to avoid costly mistakes and advocate for responsible use.
learning_outcome: Learners can identify failure risks in an AI agent setup, apply a basic safety checklist before deploying or using one, and clearly explain accountability when something goes wrong.
